title: ‘No guarantees’ on Labor’s NDIS bill
full_text:
Opposition NDIS spokeswoman Melissa McIntosh says there are “no guarantees” the Coalition will support Labor’s legislation to rein in the cost of the $50bn-a-year scheme, leaving the door open to delaying the bill even if it puts at risk the government’s growth targets.
The member for the western Sydney seat of Lindsay, which has a high proportion of NDIS participants and is being targeted by One Nation at the next election, Ms McIntosh raised concern over the prospect of people being unfairly booted off the scheme under Labor’s growth targets.
Revealing her concerns for the first time in The Australian earlier this year, Ms McIntosh has been softer than other Liberals on how aggressively to cut the scheme in the face of Labor’s plans to shave $185bn from the program over the next decade.
Ms McIntosh stood by her position on Tuesday as parliament debated the National Disability Insurance Scheme legislation, which the Coalition is due to pass in the lower house this week, after the government laid out plans to reduce scheme growth to as low as 0.2 per cent in forward estimates before normalising to about 5 per cent in the 2030s.
“I’m very concerned, because a lot of the government’s budget is based around these big cuts to the NDIS,” she said in Canberra. “I know MPs offices right around the country are getting contacted, people with real severe disability are having their plans cut without any notice, and they’re the people I’m concerned about.” An outspoken critic of NDIS cuts, Ms McIntosh added that she was “not shy of a fight” when it came to a future One Nation challenge for her electorate, for which she vowed to continue advocating. “I’m always up for a fight, and I’m happy to fight because I’m fighting for the people that I love and I care for,” she said.
The Liberal frontbencher said that on top of working closely with NDIS Minister Mark Butler on NDIS reforms, she had “had long conversations with Greens disability spokesman Jordon Steele-John, revealing the two of them “ shared some concerns”.
“You might be surprised that the Greens and the Coalition do have similar concerns in some aspects of this,” she said. “We settled on the three days (for a Senate inquiry into the bill), but if it does need to be longer, then it needs to be longer. That’s not something I’m envisaging. We’ll see what the government’s trying to push.” While she was optimistic about the snap Senate inquiry into the bill, which she championed, Ms McIntosh left the door open to amending the reforms in the upper house. “The Senate inquiry, if we can get it done in those three days … and if it goes well, then they’ll have our support,” she said. “If there are things that aren’t quite right, we’ll be making amendments.” Pressed on whether the Coalition might pursue a longer inquiry process, or even block the passage of the bill, Ms McIntosh said “there’s no guarantees”.
Any delay in the government’s timeline to implement the legislation by the end of next month would put at risk its hopes to cut scheme growth from the current rate of about 11 per cent down to about 4.5 per cent in the next financial year.
Ms McIntosh rebuffed the suggestion the Liberals would be criticised if they delayed the bill and put such growth reductions at risk, saying it was “the government that wants those outcomes … we’ve got an obligation as lawmakers to get the law right for these people,” she said. “It is definitely very much a balancing act to have compassion for people, but to have the reality that the budget is just blowing out.” Ms McIntosh, open about her ambitions to be Coalition leader earlier this year, said she was “not getting involved” with the opposition’s tactics in the Senate, as the party considers whether to use a delay in passing the NDIS legislation as a threat to stop Labor ramming through its tax package. Beyond unfair cuts to people’s NDIS budgets, Ms McIntosh raised concern over the lack of action on fraud as a weakness in the legislation.
